title: Diagnostics, System Design & the Boundary of Competence

supplementary.title: [Reference] Local building codes and permit requirements

competence_markers.description: The student can construct a failure tree for any household system and work through it systematically to diagnose a real or simulated failure
The student can calculate electrical loads on a circuit and determine whether the circuit is within safe capacity
The student can use a multimeter to measure voltage and continuity for electrical troubleshooting
The student understands redundancy and can identify single points of failure in household systems
The student can analyze cross-system impacts of a failure scenario (water damage affecting electrical, structural, and health systems)
The student can clearly articulate the boundary between work they can safely perform and work that requires a licensed professional
The student can teach how a household system works to someone with no technical background, resulting in the learner being able to explain it back

content.why_this_matters:
At Weathering Storms, you learned how household systems work and how to perform basic maintenance and repairs. Now you develop the diagnostic mind — the ability to approach an unfamiliar system failure, reason from first principles, and methodically determine what went wrong. You also learn where your competence ends and professional help begins, which may be the most important lesson in this entire sub-domain.

Diagnostic methodology is not specific to electrical or plumbing systems. The same approach — observe symptoms, form hypotheses, test systematically, eliminate possibilities — works for every complex system you will encounter: digital systems (4.2), organizational problems (4.3), financial structures (4.1), and medical symptoms. The student who can build a failure tree for a furnace that will not start can build a failure tree for a business that is losing money. The thinking transfers.

You will also learn to see systems as interconnected rather than isolated. A roof leak is not just a roofing problem — if water reaches the wiring, it becomes an electrical hazard. If it saturates the framing, it becomes a structural problem. If it promotes mold growth, it becomes a health hazard. The person who thinks in interconnected systems sees consequences that the specialist, focused on their single domain, may miss.

And you will learn to teach. When you can explain how a household system works to someone who has never thought about it — when you can make the invisible visible — you have achieved genuine mastery of the material.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Diagnostic methodology** — systematic approach to unfamiliar system failures using observation, hypothesis, testing, and elimination
2. **Failure tree construction** — mapping all possible causes of a system failure into a branching structure for systematic investigation
3. **System design thinking** — understanding how systems are designed for reliability, redundancy, and failure tolerance
4. **Cross-system integration** — seeing the interconnections between electrical, plumbing, HVAC, and structural systems
5. **The boundary of competence** — knowing precisely what you can safely do yourself and what requires a professional
6. **Teaching systems logic** — explaining how systems work to someone with no background, making the invisible visible
7. **Ohm's law and load calculations** — practical electrical calculations for circuit capacity and safety
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Diagnostic Methodology

When a complex system fails, there is a powerful temptation to guess. The furnace will not start — maybe it is the thermostat. Maybe it is the gas valve. Maybe the igniter is bad. Without a method, you try things at random, and random guessing wastes time, wastes money (replacing parts that are not broken), and can make the problem worse.

The diagnostic method replaces guessing with systematic investigation:

**Step 1: Observe the symptoms precisely.** What exactly is happening? "The furnace is not working" is a poor symptom description. "The thermostat calls for heat, the inducer motor starts, I hear clicking from the igniter area, but the burners do not light and the system locks out after three attempts" — that is a precise symptom description that immediately narrows the possibilities.

**Step 2: Establish the timeline.** When did it start? Was it gradual or sudden? What changed before it started? A furnace that stopped working during the first cold snap after summer may have a different problem than one that stopped working mid-winter after running for months. The timeline provides context.

**Step 3: Construct a failure tree.** List all possible causes of the observed symptoms. Organize them by system: electrical causes, mechanical causes, fuel/fluid causes, sensor/control causes. This is your roadmap for investigation.

**Step 4: Test the most likely and easiest-to-test hypothesis first.** If the furnace clicks but does not light, a dirty flame sensor is more likely and easier to test than a failed gas valve. Check the flame sensor first (clean it with fine sandpaper — 5 minutes). If that solves the problem, you are done. If not, move to the next hypothesis.

**Step 5: Change one variable at a time.** If you replace two parts simultaneously and the system starts working, you do not know which replacement solved the problem. You may have wasted money on the part that was not broken. Worse, if the system fails again, you do not know where to look. Discipline yourself to test one hypothesis, evaluate the result, and then — only then — move to the next.

**Step 6: Document what you find.** Write down the symptoms, the tests you performed, the results, and the solution. This documentation is valuable for two reasons: it helps you if the problem recurs, and it helps you build a mental library of failure patterns that makes future diagnosis faster.

### Part 2: Failure Trees

A failure tree is a structured map of all possible causes for a specific failure, organized so you can work through them systematically.

**Example: Furnace will not start when thermostat calls for heat.**

Branch 1 — Electrical:
- Thermostat not sending signal (dead batteries, broken wire, faulty thermostat)
- Breaker tripped or fuse blown
- Power switch on furnace turned off
- Control board failed
- Safety switch tripped (high-temperature limit, pressure switch, rollout switch)

Branch 2 — Ignition:
- Igniter failed (hot surface igniter cracked, spark igniter not sparking)
- Flame sensor dirty or failed (flame lights briefly then goes out)
- Gas valve not opening (failed valve, no signal from control board)

Branch 3 — Fuel:
- Gas supply shut off (valve closed, meter locked)
- Gas pressure insufficient

Branch 4 — Mechanical:
- Inducer motor failed (no draft established, pressure switch will not close)
- Blower motor failed (system runs but no air flow)
- Condensate drain blocked (on high-efficiency furnaces, blocked drain triggers safety shutdown)

You work through the tree starting at the top of each branch (the simplest and most common causes) before moving to the more complex and rare causes at the bottom. Most furnace failures are solved in the first two or three items checked.

**Building failure trees for other systems:** The same approach works for any system. A car that will not start has electrical branches (battery, starter, ignition), fuel branches (fuel pump, filter, injectors), and mechanical branches (compression, timing). A plumbing system with no water pressure has supply branches (shutoff valve closed, main break), pressure branches (pressure regulator failed, pressure tank failed), and obstruction branches (clogged pipe, frozen pipe).

The skill is not memorizing failure trees for every system. The skill is knowing how to build one for any system by understanding how the system works and what can go wrong at each point.

### Part 3: Ohm's Law and Load Calculations

Ohm's law is the fundamental relationship in electricity: **Voltage = Current x Resistance (V = I x R)**.

- **Voltage (V)** is electrical pressure, measured in volts. Your household outlets provide 120V.
- **Current (I)** is electrical flow, measured in amperes (amps). The amount of electricity actually flowing through a circuit.
- **Resistance (R)** is opposition to flow, measured in ohms. Every device, wire, and connection has resistance.

**Why this matters practically:** A 15-amp breaker can safely carry 15 amps of current. If you plug devices into that circuit that together draw more than 15 amps, the breaker trips. Using Ohm's law (and its derivative, Power = Voltage x Current), you can calculate whether a circuit can handle the load you are putting on it.

**Example:** A space heater rated at 1,500 watts on a 120V circuit draws 1,500 / 120 = 12.5 amps. On a 15-amp circuit, that leaves only 2.5 amps for everything else on that circuit. If someone plugs in a vacuum cleaner (10 amps) on the same circuit, the total is 22.5 amps — the breaker trips immediately. This is why the NEC limits continuous loads to 80% of breaker rating (12 amps on a 15-amp breaker) and why high-draw appliances need dedicated circuits.

**Using a multimeter:** A multimeter measures voltage (is there power here?), resistance (is this wire continuous or broken?), and current (how much electricity is flowing?). Basic multimeter skills:
- Measure voltage: Set to AC volts, place probes on hot and neutral. You should read approximately 120V at a live outlet.
- Measure resistance (continuity): Set to ohms or continuity. With the circuit de-energized, place probes across a switch — you should read near-zero ohms when the switch is closed (continuous) and infinite/OL when open (broken).
- These two measurements — "is there power here?" and "is this path continuous?" — are the foundation of all electrical troubleshooting.

### Part 4: Redundancy and Resilience in Systems

Well-designed systems anticipate failure and include redundancy — backup capabilities that keep the system functioning when a component fails.

**Examples of redundancy:**
- A backup generator maintains electrical power when the grid fails
- Dual water heaters ensure hot water even if one unit fails
- A bypass valve in a water treatment system allows untreated water to flow if the treatment system fails (water without treatment is better than no water)
- A manual override on an automatic system allows human control when the automation fails

**Designing redundancy:** When you design or modify a system, ask: "What happens when this component fails?" If the answer is "the entire system stops working," consider whether that is acceptable. For critical systems (heating in cold climates, water supply, sump pumps in flood-prone areas), redundancy is not a luxury — it is a necessity.

**Single points of failure:** A component whose failure stops the entire system is called a single point of failure. A home with one water heater has a single point of failure for hot water. A home with one sump pump has a single point of failure for flood protection. Identifying single points of failure is the first step toward building resilience.

### Part 5: Cross-System Integration

Real-world problems rarely stay within a single system. Understanding the connections between systems is what separates the competent homeowner from the specialist who sees only their own domain.

**Water and electricity:** A roof leak dripping onto a light fixture creates an electrical hazard. A burst pipe flooding a basement can short-circuit the electrical panel if water reaches it. A sump pump failure during a storm means both a flooding problem and potentially an electrical problem when water reaches outlets and appliances.

**Plumbing and structure:** A slow leak inside a wall saturates framing, promoting rot and potentially compromising structural integrity. Frozen pipes that burst can cause extensive water damage to walls, ceilings, and foundations.

**HVAC and air quality:** A cracked heat exchanger in a furnace can introduce combustion gases (including carbon monoxide) into the living space. A ductwork leak in an unconditioned space (attic, crawlspace) can draw in dust, moisture, and contaminants. Blocked condensate drains on air conditioning units can cause water damage to ceilings and walls.

**The integrated thinker's advantage:** When you observe a problem, you consider consequences across systems. A small water stain on a ceiling is not just a painting problem — it is evidence of water where it should not be, and water in the wrong place affects every system it contacts.

### Part 6: The Boundary of Competence

This may be the most important section in 2.2.5. It is certainly the most mature.

You have learned to replace switches, fixtures, and outlets. You can troubleshoot common failures. You can read your electrical panel and trace circuits. But there is a line between what you can safely and legally do yourself and what requires a licensed professional.

**Electrical boundaries:**
- You can replace existing switches, outlets, and fixtures (like-for-like replacement)
- You should not add new circuits, modify the panel, run new wiring through walls, or install sub-panels without proper knowledge, permits, and inspection
- Any work that involves the service entrance, the meter, or the main panel connections requires a licensed electrician
- In most jurisdictions, electrical work beyond basic fixture replacement requires a permit and inspection — this protects you and future occupants

**Plumbing boundaries:**
- You can replace fixtures, clear drains, replace valves, and perform maintenance
- Major modifications (adding new fixture locations, modifying drain/vent systems, working on the main sewer line) require professional knowledge and often permits
- Gas plumbing (gas lines to furnace, water heater, stove) should be handled only by qualified professionals — gas leaks are explosion hazards

**HVAC boundaries:**
- You can replace filters, clean accessible components, and perform basic troubleshooting
- Refrigerant work (charging, leak repair) is regulated and requires EPA certification
- Heat exchanger assessment, gas valve repair, and control board replacement require professional expertise

**The mature response:** Knowing when to call a professional is not admitting defeat. It is the highest form of competence — you have diagnosed the problem accurately, you know what it requires, and you have the judgment to recognize that it exceeds your capability or legal authority. The professional you call benefits from your diagnostic work — you have narrowed the problem, which saves their time and your money.

**The danger of overconfidence:** More damage is done by homeowners who think they know what they are doing than by homeowners who know they do not. A miswired circuit can start a fire years later. An improper plumbing modification can cause a sewer gas leak. A DIY electrical panel modification can create an electrocution hazard that kills someone who is not you. The stakes of getting it wrong are not cosmetic — they are potentially fatal. Respect the boundary.

### Part 7: Teaching Systems Logic

The ultimate test of your understanding is the ability to explain how a system works to someone who has never thought about it.

Choose a household system — electrical, plumbing, or HVAC — and explain it from beginning to end to someone with no technical background. Start from the source (power plant, water main, furnace) and trace the path to the point of use (outlet, faucet, register). Explain what each component does and why. Make the invisible visible.

The challenge is not technical knowledge — you have that. The challenge is translation. You must take what you know and present it in terms the listener can follow. You must use analogies (water pressure is like a garden hose — squeeze the end and pressure increases), demonstrations (show them the breaker panel and flip a breaker), and sequential logic (first this happens, which causes this, which results in this).

When the listener can explain the system back to you — in their own words, not yours — you have succeeded. And in the process of teaching, you will have deepened your own understanding, because teaching forces you to organize knowledge coherently and fill gaps you did not know you had.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Diagnostic Methodology
**Developing well:** When presented with a system failure (real or simulated), the student pauses, observes precisely, constructs a mental or written failure tree, and works through it systematically. They test the simplest and most likely causes first. They change one variable at a time. They document their findings. The process is methodical, not random.

**Developing slowly:** The student guesses, replaces parts at random, or gives up quickly. They cannot construct a failure tree or do not follow their own tree systematically.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you provided enough diagnostic practice opportunities? Have you modeled the method yourself, narrating your reasoning? Consider practicing with simpler systems first (a battery circuit with multiple possible faults) before moving to household systems.

### 2. Load Calculation and Electrical Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can calculate circuit loads accurately, use a multimeter to measure voltage and continuity, and apply Ohm's law to practical situations. They understand why breakers trip (overcurrent), why GFCIs trip (ground fault), and what the NEC 80% rule means.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot perform the calculations or use the multimeter correctly. They confuse voltage, current, and resistance.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have the mathematical foundation (1.5) for these calculations? Ohm's law requires basic algebra. If the math is weak, coordinate with the student's numeracy work.

### 3. Boundary of Competence
**Developing well:** The student can clearly articulate what they can safely do themselves versus what requires a professional. They can explain why the boundary exists (safety, code requirements, system complexity). They demonstrate the maturity to stop when they reach their limit, even when the problem is frustrating.

**Developing slowly:** The student believes they can handle anything, or conversely, believes they cannot handle anything. Neither extreme reflects the competent middle ground.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you had the boundary conversation explicitly? Have you modeled calling a professional yourself? Is the student's IC1 (Character) development adequate to support the humility required?

### 4. Cross-System Thinking
**Developing well:** The student can describe how a failure in one system affects others. They anticipate cascading consequences. When observing a problem, they consider impacts beyond the obvious.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats each system as isolated. They do not consider cross-system effects.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you discussed real-world examples of cascading failures? Have you presented scenarios that cross system boundaries? The cross-system impact analysis project is designed to develop this skill.

### 5. Teaching Effectiveness
**Developing well:** The student explains a system clearly, at the listener's level, using analogies and demonstrations. The listener can explain it back accurately. The student adapts when the listener is confused.

**Developing slowly:** The student teaches at their own level, uses jargon without explanation, or becomes frustrated when the listener does not understand.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did the student prepare the explanation in advance? Did they consider the listener's starting point? Can the student simplify without sacrificing accuracy?
